title: Cooldown applied to Uniswap pair address permanently breaks buy functionality
claim: The _transfer function applies the 60-second cooldown using _lastTx[sender] rather than _lastTx[msg.sender]. During a buy transaction, the Uniswap pair calls transfer(pair, buyer, amount), making sender equal to the pair address. Since all buy transactions share the same sender (the pair), only one buy can succeed per 60 seconds. With ownership renounced (owner() == address(0) confirmed on-chain), the pair can never be excluded from the cooldown, permanently throttling all buy transactions.
impact: The token's core trading functionality is broken: only one buy per 60 seconds can succeed globally. This creates a severe liquidity bottleneck and enables a griefing attack where an attacker submits a tiny buy every ~61 seconds to permanently block all other buyers. The cooldown cannot be disabled because _disableCooldown() is onlyOwner and ownership is renounced.
paths: 1. Any user buys tokens via Uniswap (swapExactETHForTokens). The UniswapV2Pair calls token.transfer(pairAddr, buyerAddr, amount).
2. Inside _transfer, sender = pairAddr. The cooldown check sets _lastTx[pairAddr] = block.timestamp + 60.
3. Any subsequent buy attempt within 60 seconds fails: block.timestamp > _lastTx[pairAddr] is false, triggering require(!cooldownEnabled) which reverts since cooldownEnabled=true permanently.
4. Attacker can weaponize: submit one tiny buy every ~61 seconds to permanently monopolize the buy channel, DoS-ing all other buyers.
5. No recovery possible: _disableCooldown() is onlyOwner and owner=address(0).

title: Auto-swap uses zero minimum ETH output enabling sandwich extraction of contract tokens
claim: The swapTokensForEth function calls uniswapV2Router.swapExactTokensForETHSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens with amountOutMin=0, accepting any amount of ETH in return for the contract's entire token balance. A sandwich attacker can front-run the swap with a pool-manipulating trade, causing the contract to receive near-zero ETH for its tokens, then back-run to restore the pool and profit from the price discrepancy.
impact: An attacker monitoring the mempool can identify transactions that will trigger the auto-swap (when contract token balance >= 5M _numOfTokensToExchangeForTeamDev). By sandwiching the victim transaction, the attacker extracts most of the contract's swap value, stealing ETH that should go to the team. The contract's accumulated charity tokens are sold at a manipulated near-zero price.
paths: 1. Attacker monitors mempool for transactions that push contract token balance over _numOfTokensToExchangeForTeamDev (5M tokens).
2. Attacker front-runs by executing a large swap that skews the SHOCO/ETH pool price (buying SHOCO to inflate price, or selling SHOCO to deflate it, depending on sandwich direction).
3. Victim transaction executes, triggering auto-swap: swapTokensForEth swaps contract's ENTIRE token balance with amountOutMin=0.
4. The contract receives minimal ETH due to manipulated pool ratio.
5. Attacker back-runs to restore pool price, profiting from the artificial price movement.
6. Contract's swap proceeds are stolen; accumulated charity tokens are effectively looted.

title: renounceOwnership() fails to clear _previousOwner enabling stealth ownership recovery
claim: The Ownable contract provides lock()/unlock() functions where lock() stores the owner in _previousOwner before zeroing _owner, and unlock() restores _owner = _previousOwner if called by _previousOwner. The renounceOwnership() function sets _owner=address(0) but never clears _previousOwner. If the owner called lock() at any point before renouncing, _previousOwner retains the owner address, and unlock() can be called post-renounce to reclaim ownership. On this deployment, _previousOwner is address(0) (verified via cast storage slot 1), so the exploit is not currently active — but the code defect is present and dangerous for any redeployment.
impact: If an owner first calls lock() then unlock() then renounceOwnership(), the apparent renunciation is fake: the original owner can call unlock() afterward to silently regain full control. This undermines the trust model — users believe ownership is permanently renounced but the deployer retains a backdoor. The defect also means that a compromised owner key before renounce could set _previousOwner to an attacker-controlled address for later recovery.
paths: 1. Deployer calls lock(0) → _previousOwner = deployer, _owner = address(0), _lockTime = now.
2. Deployer waits 1+ seconds, calls unlock() → _owner = deployer (ownership restored).
3. Deployer calls renounceOwnership() → _owner = address(0). _previousOwner remains deployer.
4. Community verifies owner() == address(0) and trusts ownership is renounced.
5. At any future time, deployer calls unlock() → require(_previousOwner == msg.sender) passes, require(now > _lockTime) passes. _owner = deployer. Full control reclaimed.
6. Deployer can now call onlyOwner functions: change teamDevAddress, add snipers, exclude accounts from fees, etc.
